vulnerability.title: Do Not Allow SSH Environment Options
vulnerability.description: Ensure that users are not able to override environment variables of the SSH daemon. The default SSH configuration disables environment processing. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for PermitUserEnvironment. To explicitly disable Environment options, add or correct the following /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: PermitUserEnvironment no
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/s

triage_decision.reason: Disabling PermitUserEnvironment is a standard SSH hardening step that does not affect system boot or critical services and is unlikely to lock out users.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; SSH environment variable override

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for sshd_do_not_permit_user_env (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sshd_do_not_permit_user_env). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message:
I wasn’t able to apply the remediation because every command I sent was rejected with the message

```
Connection closed by 192.168.1.221 port 22
```

This indicates that the SSH session to the target host is dropping before any shell command can run, so I can’t read the configuration files, modify them, or reload `sshd`.  

**What can be done now?**

1. Verify that the SSH service on the host is running and reachable (e.g., `ssh -vvv user@localhost`).
2. Make sure the agent tool interface is correctly configured and has permissions to execute commands on the system.
3. Once connectivity is restored, the following single‑command sequence will achieve the hardening:

```bash
mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d
sed -i '/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
for f in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf; do
    sed -i '/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b/d' "$f"
done
printf 'PermitUserEnvironment no\n' > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
chown root:root /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
systemctl reload sshd
```

If you’re unable to restore the SSH connection, let me know the specific error or condition, and I’ll help troubleshoot further.

reasoning_messages.content:
I’m sorry, but I couldn’t apply the changes because the SSH connection was closed before any of the commands could run:

```
Connection closed by 192.168.1.221 port 22
```

Without a stable connection I can’t read, modify, or write files on the host, so the remediation couldn’t be performed.  

**Please check the remote host and tool connectivity** and then let me know so I can attempt the following steps again:

1. Ensure `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d` exists (`mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`).
2. Read `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` and delete any existing `PermitUserEnvironment` lines with `sed -i '/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config`.
3. Delete the same directive from any files in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf`.
4. Create or overwrite `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` with the line `PermitUserEnvironment no\n` (mode 0600, owned by root:root).
5. Reload SSH: `systemctl reload sshd`.

If you’re able to re‑establish the connection, I can run these commands one by one as described.

reasoning_messages.content:
I couldn’t apply the remediation because the remote connection timed‑out/closed before any command could be executed:

```
Connection closed by 192.168.1.221 port 22
```

Without a live shell I can’t read, edit, or reload `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or the included files.  

**Next steps**

1. Verify that the remote host is reachable and the tool interface is working.  
2. Once the connection is re‑established, run the following (one command at a time) to enforce the hardening:

   ```bash
   mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d
   sed -i '/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b/d' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
   for f in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf; do
       sed -i '/^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b/d' "$f"
   done
   printf 'PermitUserEnvironment no\n' > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
   chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
   chown root:root /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
   systemctl reload sshd
   ```

If you’re unable to reconnect, let me know any additional details so I can try to assist further.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Removes all existing PermitUserEnvironment directives and adds a correct override in a dedicated file, so the SSH daemon will disallow user-defined environment options. The plan is safe and reversible, though reloading sshd to apply changes and ensuring a trailing newline would improve completeness.
concerns: No command shown for restarting or reloading sshd, which is required for changes to take effect.
The sed removal pattern may also remove lines that contain the directive as part of a comment if not carefully anchored.
The plan does not explicitly guarantee a terminating newline in the new config file.”
The file ownership is not explicitly set; default may not be root:root.”
suggested_improvements: Add a final step to reload sshd (e.g., systemctl reload sshd) after modifying the files.
Ensure the sed command precisely matches the directive and not commented lines, e.g., use '^\s*PermitUserEnvironment\b'.
Verify or add a trailing newline to the config file to avoid parser errors.
Explicitly set file owner to root:root for added security.”

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly enforces PermitUserEnvironment no by using an include file and removes any prior directives. It also reloads sshd to apply changes. Minor improvements can further harden the remediation.
concerns: The created config file may not guarantee a terminating newline, which can cause parsing issues.  The plan does not explicitly backup existing config files before modification.  The removal regex, while generally safe, could be over‑aggressive if the keyword appears in a multi‑word comment (e.g., '# example PermitUserEnvironment is disabled').
The permissions and ownership of the new file are mentioned but not explicitly set in a command line; adding 'chown root:root' is advisable.
suggested_improvements: Add a trailing newline to /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf (e.g., using 'printf' or appending '\n').
Back up /etc/ssh/sshd_config and any .conf files in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d before editing (e.g., cp -p).
Include 'chown root:root' after creating the file to guarantee ownership.
Consider using 'sed -i.bak' to keep a backup of each modified file.
After reloading, run 'sshd -T | grep PermitUserEnvironment' to confirm the effective value is "no".
